I took the other reviews with a grain of salt (no pun intended) and thought maybe it's a matter of taste. Well, if it is, my taste is I absolutely hate this. In the first area I mostly had fun - a bit like Monster Hunter, good pacing.

The second area was a bit iffy. Often frustrating, many unfair situations. If the signature of Soulslike games is "tough, but fair" or "if you die, it's your own fault", then this is not a Soulslike, and it begins to become evident here. Fighting multiple mages at once is fun at first but rapidly becomes a chore or a war of attrition. You attack them, maybe you die or retreat to get supplies, but it doesn't matter since most of the fights are persistent. Even so, most gear is crafted from mage parts, so you're going to be grinding. On top of this sometimes a mage may become completely entrenched on a platform with no way to climb up without being knocked back down.

By the third and fourth areas the problem was very evident: Running through the entire hub every time just to go to a different area. Too many traps that are too hard to see and too hard to dodge. Most of the traps just send you flying off a ledge, but tend to juggle you. There's also terrible platform layouts combined with frustrating controls - dodge and grapple are the same button, for example., or how grappling itself doesn't work straight up and has no direction control, or how inconsistent air attacks can be done.

I paid $10 for this but I still don't think it was worth the money. I hate to say it but my life would have been better if I didn't play this game. There's some good ideas in here for sure, but the execution is just not fun - this game is more like a job.

The game has some okay highs and some ABYSSAL downs. I'll list some of them below:

PROS:
- The gameplay is very similar to salt and sanctuary but the builds are funnier to play and I tested a lot of them (Light armor is still terrible).

- The game is short but if you take your time to look for and hunt the mages it can be quite a nice length.

- Overall exploration was good.

- Enemy variety was also good despite some repetitions here and there.

CONS:
- The Hub. The portal that takes you to other maps are SO FAR AWAY from the starting point. Why? You need to visit the hub everytime and EVERY SINGLE TIME YOU NEED TO RUN AROUND TO REACH THE PORTAL

- Overall platforming. The platform movement in this game is terrible and the game has quite a lot of them.

- FLYING F**KING ENEMIES. What's with those flying enemies that keep shooting you waaaay out of your reach and they also can fly through walls and even if you have projectiles (arrows, throwing axes etc) you STILL can't hit them if they want to hide behind a wall.

- ENEMIES EDGE GUARDING. Sometimes the enemies will walk around and will choose to stay right at the edge of a platform that you want to climb. You have iframes in the edge grab animation but the enemies will start attacking as soon as you finish the animation. If you're able to try and jump over the edge, the enemy will still hit you and whatever hits you in the air COMPLETELY removes your momentum, so you fall straight down. It is even worse when a large enemy decides to edge camp you because in this case you're COMPLETELY unable to grab the edge or jump over. You need to wait for the enemy to leave the edge or kill them with a ranged weapon. The worst situation that happened to me was a mage in temple of embers that decided to edge camp a platform that you needed to use the grappling hook. There was no place to stay on so I could fire ranged weapons. I needed to constantly jump at him and use my melee attack only to fall and take 60% of my HP several times until the mage decided to teleport.

- Enemies invading boss arenas. This one kinda explains itself.

- Traps... just traps everywhere. Traps in this game are barely visible and they're simply everywhere. The damage that they deal gave me the impression that they're not designed to exactly kill you. Instead, their focus is to simply deal a considerable amount of damage and knock you off of a platform so you need to climb aaaaalll over again.

- The mage system that is the core of the game. This is either love it or hate it because the mages are bosses that summon minions around the map and they keep teleporting around so you need to chase and kill them. The terrible thing is that they don't stay dead. They keep respawning even if you don't rest at the obelisks. Sometimes in the second map (Bol something) they even spawn exactly at the obelisk so you need to fight or avoid him right at the spawn point. And it's even worse when they decide to teleport near annoying enemies. The good news is that they'll fight each other but you still would need to wait for their fight to end or risk get ganked by all of them at once. Also it happened to me in one of the last maps (dragonsomething peak) that THREE mages spawned in the same area. It was a nightmare.

- OST is just as lacking as salt and sanctuary but the first game at least had the still palace and nameless god ost which was kinda nice. This game doesn't have a single good song

This is my review of Salt and SACRIFICE
I'm not really sure why people are giving this game a negative review based on the fact that it's not "Salt and Sanctuary"... You wouldn't yell at a chef for serving beef stew after he originally served potato stew. They both have their own merits and downfalls. You certainly can't fully discredit either for being unlike the other. That's just asinine. TL;DR: This is more of a 2D Monster Hunter set in the S+S world, and it's definitely worth playing through, especially if you have a few friends to play with or find some along the way.

~STORY~
Salt and Sacrifice is a overarching story about Sacrifice. A world plagued by mages, and the more of them you kill the more seem to keep coming up. It all ties together by the end, if you pay attention. It's a decent story considering I still can't tell you what most of Souls-likes are about.
My main troubles with the story points are my main troubles with any souls-likes with stories. I can't seem to find when or where the characters will show up in the world. That part requires a guide for me, as it has in all the Souls games as well as Elden Ring.

~GAMEPLAY~
The actual gameplay isn't what most souls-likes are. It's more of a 2D Monster Hunter game. You hunt monsters with friends, you collect materials, you craft weapons with said materials.
The gameplay is smooth as butter. You choose an initial "class" which basically just starts you off in different places on the skill tree. You have many different weapon types to choose from, a lot more than the previous entry, and it's a lot more robust in terms of skills, magic, abilities, etc.
The other awesome fact are the different "tools" you find that helps you unlock different areas. It's a great system.
My gripe with this system is that armor still has 2 types. Light and Heavy. You have to unlock them in the skill tree. But bosses can only give you 1 set. So you'll end up not able to get an armor because you wear Light Armor and not Heavy and vise versa. It doesn't makes sense in this kind of game. Armor shouldn't have been split into 2 sets like this, or behind a skill tree unlock. The game is hard enough having to upgrade all the armor it doesn't need all this extra to weigh it down.
I do love the weapon system here, even with the unlocks in the skill tree, it's a lot of fun to get new stuff and try out what their skills are.
The downside to that(or upside depending on how you look at it) is that boss skills are bound to weapons, and you don't inherently get them or anything. The upside to that is that you swap weapons and you have new stuff to use without having to bind the skills individually. The downside is that your weapon type might not come with good skills. The real tragic downside though is if you don't have the skill tree unlocked for using focus skills, then they're just dumb weapons that don't have any skills. That doesn't seem right, but it's the way it is.
The game is too damn difficult for a monster hunter game though. Like in MHW, you kill these huge monsters and they are all initially hard, but then you get better gear and they feel like "Oh yeah, I could totally trash this mob in my sleep", I found a lot of mages aren't that. Especially because they are leveled depending on the zone. So a sanguimancer will always 2-3 shot you. Despite farming him and being decked up in fully upgraded pieces of HIS armor, he'd still mop the floor with me if I wasn't immaculate in my timing. There's something so sour about that. The monsters mages spawn also go up and become really tough towards end game, despite your cool flame resistant gear or whatever, those little dragons the Dracomancer spawns can still two shot you somehow....
The skill tree: Why? I have yet to really notice if dexterity or strength do much of ANYTHING in relation to actual attack numbers. Can anyone confirm? Do they? I felt this way in the earlier entry too. I upgrade gear and that makes a difference, but it seems the game doesn't actually care or the difference is so minute it might as well not care. So as it stands it's just a way for character customization and devoting yourself to one skilltree, instead of multiple.

~ATMOSPHERE~
I'm gunna lump music, ambiance, sound, and general vibes up here.
It's a 2D platformer, so there's only so much the dev could have done to really spice things up. Random mobs don't really make a lot of noises other than their attacks. Mages are silent also. Would have loved to hear more roaring and hollering. But I guess that's because it's a multiplayer game? I dunno. With that having been said, there's nothing quite as satisfying as seeing the screen shake, the heavy guitar and drums swell as you rip the heart out of a mage and crush or devour it. That's VERY satisfying, even after the 40th mage.
All in all my play of Salt and Sacrifice was very rewarding. Having beat it I will go back to play NG+. I somehow missed two types of enemies, and that's maddening, lol. I also didn't complete any of the NPC quests. It was definitely worth it for the reduced price from having owned S+Sanctuary. Having played it doesn't make me appreciate the former any more, like some may claim. It's its own game, and deserves the respect it was built with.
P.S. Two dudes basically made this whole game... I don't see why there's all this hate for the fact it's not what they were expecting. That's sooo rude. They worked night and day on this for probably a few years, only to have a thousand die hard Sanctuary fans give it a heck tone of flack because it wasn't what they wanted.
Well, message received, if the dev bothers to make another game(or can afford to) then I'm sure S+Sanctuary 2 will come out. It's basically a different genre of game anyways :P

This game is dreadfully mediocre because it takes a few different influences and meshes them together poorly. I'm not going to elaborate on everything here, but here is my basic gist of the game.

The Good: Cooperative gameplay, weapons, items, world-building, characters.
The Mediocre: Wandering mage hunts, enemy placement, platforming.
The Bad: Single Player, PVP, character building, enemy design, perfect guards, stamina system, equipment loads

While it has been stated that the game takes influence from Monster Hunter, it's pretty much just in the way you gather items and make your weapons; otherwise it's a souls-like through and through. Most of the bad is the enemy design crapping on everything else. Most regular enemies have 1-3 attacks while mages have somewhere around 3-6. Most enemies constantly attack, and attack quickly, not leaving much room for anything really. Mages will chain attack after attack until you are dead, sometimes releasing multiple delayed ones at once (screw the sanguimancer and electromancer especially). This makes for boring, frustrating single-player gameplay, and just okay gameplay cooperatively, because playing with other people is fun.

Here are the other main gameplay problems: 

Stamina is way too easy to deplete for how quickly enemies attack. With the speed of enemies in this game, it probably would have been better to just eliminate it all together, because you will all too often find yourself out of it, while the mage releases their never ending onslaught. You also lose so much stamina failing a perfect guard, that you basically need to be perfect at it to get anywhere. There's also the fun of having a slower reaction time to doing something else on a regular guard as opposed to a perfect. Stamina punishes the player, especially when alone while enemies are free-ranged spam lords of the worst caliber. The other problem is how long it takes to break enemy guards. Even with some pretty heavy weapons, tons of enemies will guard several strikes and then send you flying with a speedy strike.

The roll is too long for how much platforming there is, and too slow if you want to wear any sort of armor without equipment load gouging. You'll fall off of many platforms rolling because it covers a ridiculous distance for the type of game this is. If you have a moderate or heavy equip load, most mages will catch you with another attack right after dodged the first one because they are too slow. Heavy characters really only work well in groups for a good chunk of the game.

Single player just seems tacked on with how the enemies are balanced. They are made to smack around groups of people, and nothing changes when you play single player. It can all be accomplished alone, sure, but it's not fun, just annoying. PVP is much the same, there are a few factions, and it's just, who can spam quicker, or who's got that poise.

Good souls-likes usually have a few flaws in some areas, but shine throughout. Salt and Sacrifice is full of irritating flaws, with the saving grace that playing with other people is enjoyable. It's the only reason I keep playing the game, other than my enjoyment of building characters. There's definitely a good game in there, but it really should have been baked and tweaked because the character speeds and enemy speeds are at war with each other. Really this game acts like it wants to be a hack and slash, but is bogged down by its souls-like and Monster Hunter influence. If they really just slowed down the enemies a bit, it would be much better. It's obviously balanced for multiplayer, but I'd prefer an overly easy multiplayer experience and a relatively tough single-player over what it is: A pretty easy multiplayer, with a hot garbage annoying single-player experience.

This game only gets a recommendation because I'm still playing it for jolly cooperation.

This game is good. Is it Sanctuary good? No. But it's good. most of my problems lay with the fact that a lot of combats feel similar. While I didn't get a lot into the Runic Arts part of combat, the movesets of the weapons were somehow both too fast and too slow with little variation between moves. I also feel like this game isn't Salt and Sanctuary 2, but rather a totally different game that drew some inspiration from the original. When I bought this game, I expected something that expanded on the concepts of the first game, but got something totally separate. The familiar weighty combat has been traded for flashy movesets, and the beautifully crafted world disappeared in place of a level-based game, more like Demon's Souls than anything. The enemies are numerous and hit hard, and a lot of the bosses have frustrating, screen-filling attacks that shred your health.
While you may think that this all amounts to a negative experience, I found the thing that fixes it all: Multiplayer. Having another Inquisitor joining you on your journey turns this frustrating game into a wonderful time with a friend, and allows you to revel in the flashiness and charm. Not only this, but it allows you to split a boss's attention, effectively negating the aggressiveness of enemies. A Souls-Like experience is traded for a Monster Hunter-esque bananza, and anyone who is a fan of local or online multiplayer needs to try this game out. 7/10

I like this game. It's interesting, it does a lot to differentiate itself from it's predecessor. I think a lot of the bad rap this game gets is overblown, with people thinking it just should have been a pure continuation of the previous title with slightly polished combat, comparing to the Dark Souls franchise. It's better to think of this as a totally different game with the same perspective and some shared elements. Going into this game on it's own merits, it's a fun little time, fighting bosses, doing the multiplayer stuff. For 20 bucks, it's worth it.

This game deserves a bit more love. Sure it lacks the edge and angst of Salt & Sanctuary and the crafting system is not up the ante of everyone, but it really plays great. 
Grotesque designs are still there, so is platforming. Yes, parries and shield + weapon are gone, also some of the wacky weapons are not here (not that there isn't different ones in their space - you can get a chainsword for example). I will say i miss the Sanctuary music. This one feels too... Mellow. And also there is about a single track for everything. One for mage combat, one for boss combat, one for each map (or none at all at times). Honestly pasting Sanctuary music in here would do it great.
Gameplay wise in my opinion is pretty fun, so is exploration. Areas are not too sizeable, which doesn't make the game drag on, and fights themselves... Well they could always be better, but if you can secure first hit on standard mobs you should be fine. I did enjoy variety of mages and coming up with what which does based on elemental damage splits. Normal bosses were... Okay. Nothing special unfortunately.
Give it a chance if you think like you might enjoy it, or played Salt & Sanctuary. Do remember to also check Salt & Sanctuary, both of these games are quite awesome.

It's good at a basic level, but dragged down by so many abstract gameplay decisions that turn trying to play it into a struggle as the game itself punches you in the balls with incredibly abstract, Lynchian mechanical ideas that didn't even really need to divert from the original as hard as it does. I don't have a problem with the basic ideas or the gameplay loop, but the game really throws the baby out down the stairs while drinking the bath water. Losing health permanently until you use a humanity equivalent is just blatantly outdated, even at this game's first release. There's no benefit to the system besides to arbitrarily twist your ♥♥♥♥. Enemy attack patterns are obfuscated, speed blitz nightmare fests where they just wildly flail at you until you die. Even more so for most bosses who have so little space between attacks that all hit like 67% of the screen that it's a hit trade simulator until somebody dies. Heavy armor, ooh boy. It's a slog, with how capacity upgrades in the skill tree only increase your weight by ONE (1) point, which isn't nearly enough to actually wear anything worthwhile without spending hours grinding out one stat. Poise is a joke, the smallest whiff of a gnarly fart will tekken floorbounce you like a bronze Xiaoyu main getting your ♥♥♥♥ rocked by a nolife Geese Howard, regardless of how big your armor is. There's literally no reason to wear anything bigger than a paper plate duct taped to party city chainmail, you'll just die anyway. The game wants to encourage blocking, but the block cost is like actually all of your stamina if you block a lethargic overhead. It's an unintuitive, unimpressive, unengaging playstyle to even attempt. And the map design is annoying with no minimap or overall map, I couldn't imagine flagellating myself with this game's level design at launch before fast travel. Only buy if you're so hungry for something like Sanctuary that you'll literally eat trash shaped like the Sodden Knight.

If you loved Salt and Sanctuary and want more, get it. If you haven't played Salt and Sanctuary, start with that. I wish I could leave a neutral review- it's not a bad game, but it's worse than its predecessor. A lot of the negative reviews are before the most recent update. I sincerely hope the developer keeps at it.

Downgrades:

[list]
[*] Builds are very similar. There is a ranged weapon for every offense stat, and most weapons come with spells.
[*] Bosses are less memorable, and more tedious. Most of the bosses are mages with some variety, and most of the fight is chasing them.
[*] Tons more backtracking. Exploration is less enjoyable- most of the time you'll hit a locked door telling you to kill more mages first.
[*] Fights are more war-of-attrition than finesse. Many bosses have very little break between attacks. I staggered a lot of bosses during big attacks, and would get punished since the boss' stagger recovery was faster than the boss' attack recovery. I think perfect guarding (even without a shield) might be the intended approach, but I just abused ranged options.
[/list]

Tips:

[list]
[*] Offensive stats hit a soft cap very early, at 25 points on most weapons. I was level 90 something at the end of the game, and I think I hit the soft cap on my primary stat around level 30. I maxed my secondary scaling and pumped defense after that.
[*] When a boss has a glowy thing on the head, that means you can grapple it for a weakspot hit
[*] If you're losing money on death, that means you're out of a resource and it's auto-buying at a small markup
[*] Don't feel bad abusing ranged weapons
[/list]

I haven't played prior versions, but the game presently post patches is plenty of fun. It's not Salt and Sanctuary 2, but Salt and Sacrifice, so I expected as much; something familiar but not a sequel. So I treated it as such, and really enjoyed my time with it.

Honestly I was very surprised, I went in with an open mind and thought it was more familiar than not - there's plenty of exploration, backtracking with new mobility options - the usual fare. I played three characters somewhat simultaneously, all different builds but keeping their progression generally the same so I could see the differences between builds. They all have their ups and downs, but I felt that stronger two-handed weapons are unmatched in damage output. Heavy armor and poise are also pretty fun to use together in both tanking and attacking through enemy hits.

Mage hunts are fun, and an interesting addition to the formula. Because the enemies they (infinitely, until killed) spawn alongside them also drop crafting materials, I never had to really "farm" a mage to make whatever gear I was looking for. This meant they didn't overstay their welcome / get boring. Personally it was interesting for me to see what designs were coming next.

There were a couple minor frustrations, but definitely nothing enough to soil the whole experience. Most issues I had just came from seemingly-odd late-game difficulty scalings. Lookout for the [spoiler] Nameless Diablomancer [/spoiler], that's quite a rude fight because of how small the arena ends up being, coupled with the enemy's tendency to create a lot of area denial and very high damage output. It's an optional boss, so it gets some leeway with me but was far and away the most frustrating encounter.

Loved the music, I was surprised to hear that Mr. Silva didn't score it; Maclaine Diemer did a pretty good job of doing his own thing but keeping enough of that Ska Studios sound in there that I wasn't sure at first.

Ok, first of all I've played only Dark Souls Remastered and Salt and Sanctuary and no other Soulsborne titles:

Well, for starters, everyone thinking that Salt and Sancrifice is just another Soulsborne Carbon Copy; it's not. This isn't even Salt and Sanctuary 2, it may take place in the same universe, but it's no sequel. It's a stand alone game.

In my opinion it's more of a 2D Monster Hunter with Bloodborne elements than a general Soulslike, but what do I mean by that? You hunt mages across certain levels and/or stages instead of beasts, with replenishable health items and ammo. The equipment crafted from materials gathered from mages possess attributes affiliated with said mage and most weapons have unique skills.

While mentioning weapons, yes, only one weapon category comes with a shield and it's not exactly the most fun one, but blocking is not exclusive to shields and even though I have no on-hand experience, but I believe Bloodborne is also played without a shield.

I've experienced a lot of the things many people have criticized about the game, like being juggled by enemies in mid-air, or being absolutely yeeted off of platforms, but in hindsight, after beating the game multiple times, that's pretty much just the Aeromancer. I'm not sure if people only played until that boss and just rage-quitted or anything, but of course there are other bosses like the Terramancers infamous bodycheck follow-up, or the Umbramancers quadruple shockwaves, or the Diablomancers relentless traps, but nothing really I couldn't handle and I'm not exactly the best player out there.
Also, it's not like you've never been juggled or yeeted in Sanctuary before, just let me remind you of the Tree of Men, the Unskinnd and the Architect or the Knight, Judge and King. Not to mention the B**** of the Lake. Or just the Spindlebeasts.

Another thing I've noticed is how much people revere Salt and Sanctuary, myself included, so after beating Sacrifice I replayed Sanctuary on enhanced mode to compare the two not just from memory:

Walking speed is the first thing I noticed. In Sanctuary your speed depends heavily on your weight, while in Sacrifice not so much, but you move slower in general, except for sprinting, which is faster than anything in Sanctuary. There are other minor things, like wall-jumping feels better in sanctuary, but platforming in general felt smoother in sacrifice. I miss the mid-air dash from Sanctuary and the interconnected level design, but I love having a hub world where my stash, a smith and merchants are. Sometimes the controls aren't fast enough in Sacrifice, like I hit LB to drink after rolling and it just wouldn't do it, but Sanctuary wasn't any better in that regard either. Also people seem to complain about the random wandering mages you can find while exploring, sometimes even multiple ones at the same time and all I can say is; the more the merrier.

I could go on, but who'd read all that? I love Salt and Sanctuary and Sacrifice alike, also, and that is just a thought, people complain about the two games not being similar enough, but how would we have reacted to Sacrifice just being a copy and paste of Sanctuary?

Massive downgrade compared to Salt and Sanctuary. The devs wanted to try too many new things all at once and EPIC (store) failed in most of them. The following review assume you have played the first game but some points make sense even for someone approaching this title for the first time. I'm going to stress some points very hard for the simple reason I've read reviews of the game before it was published on steam and boy do I wish I took them to heart

Graphic is better, nothing to complain here and the use of colours and shades is far superior to Sanctuary. Hands down an improvement with no drawbacks 

Sound is good even if I like the BG of Sanctuary better but to each their own tastes. Each hit against shields sound "real" enough and the only one I really didn't like is the effect of lightning "magic" (again, you may like it, I didn't)

Now for the plethora of flaws I really could not recommend the game. Many of these things have already been stressed by other players in their reviews and I wish I took them seriously enough but alas....

- Forget weapon selection the way you knew it, want to use a specific weapon with a shield you like? NOPE, they come paired and there's nothing you can do about it.
- Weapons are now tied to some specific elements and grades but they are fixed and cannot be altered in any way. Wanted to use a lightning greatsword in endgame? Think again. Oh and you also have to check if the weapon uses divine or arcane weapon arts since they need to be unlocked separately. It's a very restricitve system that massively limit build variety
- Many players complained about how asinine was to assign weapons to a grade and being unable to use them unless you got their badge on the skill tree. You can have 40 strenght and being able to lift the best greatsword in the game but forget using the most basic club if you don't unlock it first
- No weapon combo anymore unless for a basic system of light/strong attack.

Now we get to the really bad stuff, those design choices that really left me staring at the screen trying to understand if I actually saw that happening

- Some levels have a LOT of verticality, like a LOT LOT. No big deal if not for the fact that most enemies abuse the living ungodly hell of knockback and ragdoll. I'm confident in your intellective prowess to understand where I'm going or rather where you'll be plummeting 
- As I was saying, most enemies spam knockback and ragdoll attacks and can and totally will juggle you mid air. Don't believe me? The juggling damage alone has been nerfed twice, yes it was that freaking bad
- Enemies have unlimited stamina BY THE WAY. Some players will tell you they don't but they do, they are just conveniently referring to the stamina to stagger them if you block them at exact time they hit you and you also need to block/parry multiple times to deplete their pseduo stamina in order to make a risposte. This mean that EVERY single enemy in the game can keep attacking indefinitely without pause 
- Related to the previous point but deserving of a spot of its own, enemies spam attack constantly. Not a figure of speech and I wrote "spam attacks" becuase it's what I meant. Bosses will be able to cast multiple attacks before the animation of the first one even completed. No, I'm not joking

I'm not even going to complain about chasing bosses around the map, that is forgivable...completely stupid since they teleport after 3 or 4 hits but that is really a minor problem compared the others.

Ah yes, this has been fixed but I feel like I have to warn potential buyers. The game started with no sorting option for inventory despite the a vast amount of players complaining about it in the first one. they probably copy pasted from the source code of the first game...Never heard of a single game besides this with no way to sort inventory. Draw your own conclusions. I really cannot recommend this game in good conscience

I will give it a 7.5/10. The game is OK, there are some up and down during the journey.

The main object of the game is Mage Hunt. The mage hunt system is quite novel, although has flawed implementation. To put it simple, you have to chase a boss (called mage in this game) as they run and summon mob, several times, until they decided to stop and a normal bossfight start. Then the loot/material drop from the loot can be used to craft equipments. Very similar to Monster Hunter.

Control is kinda good. Smooth movement and combat. The skill tree and different weapon types give a lot of replayability. Platforming has some section where it feel too tight/unfair. I've played this for a while now, and while yes, it's not really a Salt and Sanctuary 2, it still is fun in its own right.

So hear me out for a bit.
Salt and Sanctuary is among my top 5 favourite games, Sacrifice? not quite, but it is a game that I enjoy and would see myself playing with other equipment sets, as I did with Sanctuary.
So yeah, I'd agree that this didn't need to be directly linked to Sanctuary, as it probably made people think that it would be the same gameplay wise, which it isn't. At it's core it is a Soulslike yeah, but the inventory and Monter Hunter elements are what make it different, instead of a single fully connected world that you explore, you have a hub area, from which you can go to 5 (At least as far as I've seen, haven't beaten the game yet, but I'm close) Areas, which I'd say are love or hate, I'm fond of them.

So here I'll list things that were in Sanctuary but won't be here
- Sanctuaries as a whole, NPCs are found in areas and only then do they come to the main hub
- Since there are no Sanctuaries, there are no Creeds or Religions.
- AIR DASH (smh on this one)
- Salt Charged enemies after you die to them
- Weapon Transmutation
- Standard Parry ( Changed for a "block just as you're being hit" way less op imo)
- And other stuff I can't recall atm

Now, stuff that Sacrifice introduces (That you might consider good)
- Actual Online (Co-op, Invasions, Temporal Help, PVP stuff, lots of ways to go about it)
- Greater inventory slots, and 2 new equipment slots for a necklace and knife
- Better (in my opinion) Ranged Weapon System ( Ranged weapons use their own slots, and are not shot until you press the attack button, also more variety on the kinds of weapons)
- Major Focus on Elemental Damages
- Running (If you run outside of combat it doesn't consume stamina, this small detail is so delightful, because I've never seen it before)
- Option of setting a checkpoint without resetting the enemies
- Storage for items

In conclusion, is Sacrifice a bad game? No, I don't think so.
Does it pale in comparison to Sanctuary? Yes, the tones feel completely different, and Sanctuary made more of an impact.
But as stated before, the gameplay differs quite a bit from Sanctuary, so within its own right, it is a fun a cool game.

All things said though, I do want a Sanctuary 2, hope the folks at Ska Studios don't forget about the old ways, if they want to make another one of these, that's fine by me, but just, also keep the Dishwasher/Sanctuary like Games please.

First and foremost, the game is fun. It scratches that same itch the first one did and adds the mage hunt mechanic on top. 
There is a lot of content and it's a blast when played on coop.

The Epic Store exclusivity deal was a slap in the face. Don't do that again, Ska Studios.

With that out of the way, the game is good. Great, even. More than a worthy sequel to Salt & Sanctuary.

There's your metroidvania-style exploration, tool acquisition opening up more exploration, non-linear progression with some backtracking and such. A lot of platforming. Done well, some good challenges and overall interestingly designed areas to traverse, but with that said: Prepare for bullcrap. Some of it optional, much of it not. You will, in all likelihood, be knocked off of high places more than once, which often kills you outright and can potentially cost you a lot of salt (experience) and silver (semi-useless currency), though if it should happen, in fairness, you can always get more of both later if you need it. Salt can be recovered from where you died, unless you die again; silver, you lose half of what you were carrying... so spend it ASAP, after you unlock some merchants, and don't worry about it until then.

There's also your 2D-ified soulsy combat, with blocking, timely block-parries, parry counter-crits (underwhelming in this game, to be honest, and they don't work at all on a lot of enemies) and a lot of rolling away from devastating blows, blasts and traps. Also you can hit things with your own weapon now and then, if you time it right. You die, learn, die, get better, and eventually you die less. Your character gets very incrementally stronger but the slope is as long as it is slow; along your journey, you can grow more than strong enough to beat down whatever you like.

Into this ties the gathering, crafting and upgrading. There are materials used for healing potion charges and ranged weapon ammunition replenishment, kind of monster hunter vibes there. Also you track down bizarre, towering mages, fight them and absorb their essence, while also collecting some crafting and upgrade materials. Loads of different mages, which you chase down, fighting their minions as you go, until they make their final stand. There are non-mage boss battles, but most bosses in the game are mages; there's great variety among them, though, and each brings its own attack patterns and minion types. From mage and minion materials, you craft weapons, armor, accessories... all kinds of neat things that grant a variety of protection types, attribute boosts, special and active abilities. Example: A fiery Highblade (katana) can, with the appropriate character ability learned, ignite its blade in order to apply burning damage over time as it strikes. Combine that with a ring of fast stamina recovery and an amulet of fast climbing, and you begin to see the possibilities.

All in all, the game is a rich and rewarding experience - if you're prepared to go in with the necessary zen mentality of accepting death as part of the cycle of learning and all that.
Highly recommended for fans of the original, or anyone wanting to try a fun, oddball mix of Dark Souls, Monster Hunter and Castlevania spanning several vast, darkly beautiful realms.

- Mages spamming unavoidable spells / AOEs
- Mobs and Mages spamming juggles
- Mobs and Mages spamming stuns
- Mages spamming summons that prevent damage
- Said summons being covered by the now immortal mage
- Running around in slow mo trying to chase said summons while all of the above and below goes on

- Enemies jumping 3/4 of the screen
- Enemies jumping 3/4 of the screen through solid walls
- Enemies floating / flying all over the screen and attacking you through solid walls (for real)
- Enemies now being invisible because they are floating / flying through "black" solid walls, all the while attacking you with spells or melee, while all of the above and below goes on
- Bonus: Enemies can juggle you through rolls and walls

- Enemies have infinite stamina and projectiles/ fp
- Enemies stuns, grabs, and juggles have no cooldown 
- Enemies stuns, grabs, and juggles can be chained by single enemies or groups
- Enemies can block 100% of damage (minus 2 statuses that deal low damage over time)
- Enemies will chase you across the whole map if they can pathfind a way, and chase you over other aggressors
- A bat bites you for 1/4 of your HP at level 55 and wearing a full heavy set (like, wtf)

- Your character moves like underwater, and the world is on speed (regardless of weight)
- Clunky controls: you try to "jump down" to get on a lower platform, but you jump up, possibly into a trap...GG
- Drinking a potion takes way too long for way too little gains + sitting duck syndrome
- Your auto-targeting abilities can and will prioritise the mob half a screen away / entirely out of screen rather than the boss mauling your pretty face off
- Rolling has both less frames and range than incoming attacks / chains of attacks, do your math

- Enemies that guard ledges and hit you as you try to land/ grab on them so you fall to unavoidable death
- Enemies that teleport you into burning chairs that deal high damage (as the hanging men is the older game, but worse due to mob gank squads)
- You cannot "aim" at an enemy, the first one in line on a 2D plane gets the bullet

- Later weapons (tier 3+) may have way worse skills than early/ mid-game variations, i.e. projectiles that will very rarely hit an enemy and for insignificant damage
- This is especially bad considering the whole game is based on heavy grinding, and it fails to reward the player for the time invested
- Heavy weapons force you to trade in a game where trading hits is a one-way ticket to stunlock-landia and reload-screen-o-landia

- A number of cheats for enemies and limitations that are only applicable to the player

- Dead PVE / PVP in a game -poorly- balanced around multiplayer (i.e. aggro management and pal revives)

- Chasing mages through multiple sections of trap-filled, ganking-mobs infested, and bad platforming-heavy levels gets very old very fast; especially when each of these can and will one-shot you one way or the other + all of the above goes on, resulting in unavoidable death

- So much deaf and relentless obnoxiousness that you won't stop playing for any other reason that you just don't care, you are not having fun, and you would rather do anything else, including the dishes

The Tireless Exalted and afterwards is one shot galore. Witch of the lake style. 
Finished Sanctuary and still enjoy it after 50+ hours. 
Here, I stopped having fun in 15 already.
It's like they won't listen the community no matter what.
Do you know the term "bait and switch"? You get five, maybe ten decent hours, then player trolling without irony.
Quite possibly the worst experience I had in 30+ years of gaming (and I am a Souls enthusiast). It's not that the game is just "hard" (although all of the above doesn't make it easy); it's that is pure tedium and BS unending.
It's a game where RNG is left unchecked, no cooldowns, no logic, so, rather than learning, you are getting either more or less lucky (oh, I got juggled/ grabbed through a roll/ one-shotted/ thrown to unavoidable death, ok, maybe next time I won't).
I would like SKA to fix it, but you can't fix stupid and I cannot find another cause for the shameless mess I am putting up with.
Since I bought it, I may as well try to power through and update my review; but you please learn from it.

Following the previous beloved tale of "Sodium Deficient Dude Kills God"...

We join yet another severely sodium deprived dude who has gotten so desperate to get their fix, they decide to commit a random crime, and because they are sodium deprived, they screw it up and get caught. The penalty is one of two options: enlist in special forces, or die. Quite the difficult choice, there...

Our protagonist chooses the former, and their mission is then given to them: There's a bunch of mages screwing up the ecosystem! They are screwing it up so bad the game has become less of a soulslike and more of a Monster Hunter World-like! So it is our protagonist's job to.....KILL EVERYTHING THAT MOVES!

As our hero/heroine dwindles the numbers of mages, the gods that are in charge of developing this world suddenly realize they were supposed to make a soulslike, and crank the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ meter to 11 as you approach endgame! We're talking juggling, one hit kills, quick moving bosses demanding precision reactions, and more move spamming than a Down-B Kirby in Smash Bros.

And then you kill Gwyn and either link the flame and make the gods feel special or kill it and piss them off something fierce. GG.

I don't think it's horrible, but I also came in knowing it was not going to be very faithful to the original. (Thanks previous steam reviewers!) If you want my honest opinion, it's an acquired taste, but it at least warrants one playthrough. Just to, at least somewhat, scratch that itch that Salt and Sanctuary left us with.

Rating: Dark Souls II/10

As a big fan of Salt & Sanctuary (played multiple runs all in co-op with my partner), I was initially a bit worried hearing that Salt & Sacrifice was apparently a bit different. I am very pleased to say that it's an incredible sequel, a very interesting blend of Soulslike (similar to the first) and Monster Hunter (a series I have no experience with).

In my time with the game so far I'm having a blast, the systems are engaging and the loop is very enjoyable. Other reviews made me think it would be a massive departure from the first, and it is different, but there's less different than I was expecting. Overall, progression isn't tightly tied to Mage Hunts anyway, so there's no mandated grind.

If you liked the first, I think you'll have a lot of fun with this one! Keep in mind though, I'm playing with online co-op (which has been flawless), and that has added a lot of fun as we discuss builds and where to explore next. Some bosses might be less enjoyable solo, but going hunting Mages together & exploring has been really great!

Wishing all the best to the developers, thank you for a great game :)
Since the quality of life updates, this game had gotten even better. Furthermore, I can hop in for a small session and leave without feeling like I didnt get anything done.

TL;DR
The game's good.
But takes some getting used to.

I'll get this out of the way first, this isn't a Sanctuary 2 like I know a lot of us where hoping it'd be. And if you go into this game right after Sanctuary, you'll get hit with a learning curve. It's not steep, but it is noticeable. And I'll just say, gone are they days of face-♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ the big monster bosses to death before the second phase starts.

I'll get the meanest issue I had out of they way first.
Mage-Fights.
Most of what you'll be fighting boss-wise are the mages, and you'll be fighting A LOT of them of all shapes and sizes.
Unfortunately, while they all look different, they each follow the same pattern of spawning a few minions for you to kill, before running away a couple times before the real boss-fight starts, which's where the monster-hunter aspect you may've heard about comes into play. And the most thinking you'll do in in these "chase phases" of each fight, is leading other monsters/mages in to pull aggro from the main mage you're hunting. But that's about it.
Unlike most of their scripted, non-mage counterparts there isn't much strategy to fighting the mages themselves. Other than keep dodging or blocking attacks until there's a gap in their spell-spam. And if possible exploit their elemental weakness.
Killing the mages and their minions gets you your materials for elemental themed weapons and gear. And one of the few aspects I actually like from fighting the mages. You'll get a bit of dialogue from each one before they truely die. Ranging form angry last words, to crumbs of the overall story.

The other thing that really grated on my nerve was the platforming. It all works 99% of the time, but ever now and then you won't grab a ledge, the grappling hook won't grapple, or that stupid sail-cloth won't work and you'll plummet to your death... This was an issue in Sanctuary too. Fall damage in 2D games is always annoying, andIt's upsetting this wasn't addressed in some way. (At least the wall-jump is built in from the start this time)
Especially when your platforming around those ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ green knights, that can jump almost anywhere and EVERY hit from their glaives send you flying! >:(
♥♥♥♥ the Mireguard, I'm glad their stupid swamp was destroyed!

Also there's still no dedicated map mechanic, and more that once I've had to google one to remember a quicker route somewhere.

With the negative stuff out of the way, I'll move on to what I actually like in the game

Exploring each area from top to bottom is just as, if not more so rewarding than before. Knowing the terrain you'll be fighting on when hunting mages can at times give you an advantage over them. And there's enough landmark-like locations so you're less likely to get lost. That said, a map will defiantly help with subsequent playthroughs in getting to latter stages faster.

Combat's just as enjoyable as before, and the dedicated ranged weapons are a nice change. No longer will you have to lug around a bow in your second weapons slot just to pull enemies out one at a time.
Early game magic isn't limited to just fire and lightning spells. And if you see a mage use a spell, chances are YOU'LL be able to use that same spell with one of the weapons you craft from the materials they drop.
So all and all, the weapons are way more diverse, and fun to play around with.

Wish I could say the same for armor...
The old system is still here, and heavy armor is almost always more of a hinderance than it's light counterpart. Not taking hit-stun with poise is cool and all, but avoiding damage altogether is just always going to feel better. And this next point is a nit-pick, but design wise I don't care for most of the new armors. Fashion-Souls this is not.
I suspect this's due to the new PvP aspects of the game, and being able to know exactly what your up against when you get invated/hop into the arena. But I can't say that for sure.

This one is small, but the inclusion of more NPC's dotted around the explorable zones in a nice touch. Even if most of them are PvP faction people. Really makes the world you're in feel alive. And a place you're actively fighting to save. Even if in typical souls-like fashion, all seems lost.

Story-wise it runs the same vein as Sanctuary. Bits of lore scattered though item/enemy descriptions, and short story segments in the skill tree. There's a few things hinted at in the Traitor's Tomb "DLC" area that came out recently that may or may not elude to your created-character not just being some random criminal off the street. But it's nothing concrete, and you're meant to draw your own conclusions about it.

Overall, if you liked Sanctuary you'll probably like Sacrifice. 
But be warned, it's not a direct sequel, there's less Dark-Souls in the formula this time around, and the grind for certain weapons/skills/equipment is real.

Also don't ask my about the PvP/Online stuff. I haven't bothered with it, and don't plan to.
That ♥♥♥♥'s for try-hards.
An improvement in combat and gameplay compared to the original, though the story feels weaker.
A Fun enough experimental multiplayer experience for the salt series. While it could have been better in many places, the emphasis on chaotic combat for a souls-like makes it unique to say the least.
Thx for a good game guys.For me it is a best part of salt title.Waiting for 3rd part.

[h1]A Prime Example Of "Mashing Genres Together Won't Automatically Make Things Better"[/h1]

I fail to understand the thought process behind making one of the best indie souls-like into a monster-hunter-like. Actually, the game is labeled as "Monster-Hunter-like, Souls-like Metroidvania". I think to myself, maybe the developers find a way to mingle the merits from the three of them. After all, Salt And Sanctuary is a great souls-like metroidvania and probably the pioneer of this sub-genre. The result begs to differ, negatively. Salt and Sacrifice is a chimera. Developers borrow a lot of core ideas from the three genres, stitch them together, and somehow fail to breathe life into it. It's a shame because I really want to like this game. I like the iconic ska studio art style, and the animation is way smoother than Salt And Sanctuary. Everything else ruins the whole experience.

[h1]Things I Like About Salt And Sacrifice:[/h1]

[b]Minor Compliments[/b]
→The iconic ska art style.
→I like the mages and their lackeys' design.
→Unique and fancy weapons, armors and trinkets to craft.
→I guess Perfect Block is now a new standard for souls-like.
→Seeing mages fighting each other is oddly satisfying.

[h1]Things I Dislike About Salt And Sacrifice:[/h1]

[b]1. The Chimera Problem[/b]
Like I said before, mashing genres together won't automatically make the game fun to play. You need to have the right mechanic and design to glue the features together.
→[b]In terms of monster-hunter-like[/b]: first, hunting mages does not feel good. Instead of you giving it all to take down a powerful being, the hunt plays out more like fighting the lackeys it summons until the mage stops running. The whole process before the showdown feels like a slog designing to stretch the length of the fight as much as possible; second, there is no real "hunting" involved. You find the residue the mage left behind, you accept the mission, and then you follow the guiding smoke till you find the culprit. No tracking. No collecting information. Just say yes to the mission and you are set; last, the most important part which was left behind, skills and set bonus each armor provides. I know the traditional level system and skill tree exist, but the lacking of those build varieties is such a missed opportunity to encourage players to hunt more mages and try out different build combinations.
→[b]In terms of metroidvania[/b]: This is not even a downgrade, Salt And Sacrifice straight up ditches the level design in favor of the monster-hunter-like gameplay. None of the great design from previous title can be seen here. There is no actual mobility to unlock, and naturally there is no platforming or bosses' attacks to incentivize players to use those abilities more. The so-called gating abilities in this game are all just glorified keys to unlock shortcuts or activate hidden platforms. This issue won't be such hassle if the level design can properly use them. Alas, it doesn't.
→[b]In terms of souls-like[/b]: this is more of a rant about design philosophy rather than gameplay mechanic. Often time when other games try to imitate the magic of the Souls-series, they always focus on the gameplay only. Of course gameplay is a core feature, but other things are just as important. Coherent item descriptions, logical enemy placements, environmental story-telling, the list goes on and on. Somehow Salt And Sacrifice throws everything away. Item descriptions of the equipment provides nothing vital clues or some interesting story. Most of them feel like they were assembled by random word generator. Bosses have no identity at all since they don't even drop their equipment when they're defeated, therefore no lore to read through. The Mages do not fare well either. Despite the cool design, their roaming nature and the lacking of dedicated levels make them more of elite mobs rather than significant bosses. 

[b]2. Other Complaints[/b]
→Locking all the weapon ability behind one or two stat requirement is not a good idea.
→Collecting resource to craft health potion does not fit souls-like.
→Enemies late-game deal absurdly high damage.
→The geyser/glider ability feels awkward to use.
→A proper map will make navigating the world less confusing and frustrating.

review: Is Salt and Sacrifice as good as Salt and Sanctuary? No. is it still a good game in its own right? Yes.

Who's ever idea was to add a cat and mouse chase mechanical to almost every boss? Go f**k yourself. 

With that noted, I still recommend the game and like it. It's challenging, it'll actually make you use your brain and not mindlessly button smash. Gotta dodge and time it, bro. Otherwise how else will you git good? Get it on sale if you feel attempted but still unsure to commit.

This game is not Salt and Sanctuary 2. It doesn't even feel like a game made by the same creators. They took a good formula for a fun game and tried to turn it into an MMO evidently, because that's what this game feels like. Sacrifice isn't a soulslike, it's a grindy mess that doesn't seem like it knows what it wants to be, lazily reused mage bosses, and removed multiple features from Sanctuary that made the gameplay feel smooth. The world is no longer interconnected and now operates on a fast travel system that just feels lazy. Multiplayer is pushed way more in this game than Sanctuary, and considering how small this game is, I spent 20 minutes trying to invade one person and eventually gave up.

TL;DR
If you wanted Salt and Sanctuary 2, don't waste your money

This is the best two player co-op we've played since Broforce! Challenging, fun, great monster design, and a lot of depth for unlocking areas and items. We played the previous one too, but prefer this one because we can play co-op. Only 2 suggestions for improving, blur effect gets a bit much, and sometimes there's not enough info displayed for items (e.g. when selling or transferring to chest). Cheers!
Upgraded combat from Sanctuary, mix in some Monster Hunter mechanics, and a cool new storyline.

Nice game, doesn't deserve the hate it's getting. It's not Salt & Sanctuary 2, so don't go in expecting it to be. Still mainly a Souls-like with the combat and build making systems being pretty much the same as in Sanctuary, but with a few Hunter-like elements thrown in. Sanctuary might be 2D Dark Souls, but this one isn't 2D Monster Hunter, not at all, so don't go in expecting that either. It's its own thing, a bit experimental I'd say, but it turned out well.

[h3]About the other reviews...[/h3]

A lot of the issues other people complain about have been fixed with the latest update. Or maybe never existed in the first place? Idk, I only started playing recently. Bosses juggling you around isn't really something that happens. There are a few combo attacks, but they usually deal around the same damage in total as a single normal attack. With some of these reviews I wonder if people even know that you have iframes on rolls, because honestly, they're making it out to be a way bigger deal than it is. The biggest danger is getting stunlocked because multiple enemies attack you at the same time or getting punched off a cliff (although the fall damage is extremely forgiving, you gotta be in free fall for a couple of seconds for it to be lethal). And while multiple enemies can swarm you, they will also fight each other, so just use that to your advantage. 75% of the time enemies will be pre-occupied when you arrive and you can just attack from behind.

[h3]Gameplay[/h3]

As for the gameplay, basically there are 5 different locations, each being a big and open map with various sub-areas that are interconnected like in Sanctuary. But the main locations are disconnected and accessed via a hub, like in Demon's Souls. The exploration is very similar to Sanctuary, with some backtracking to access pathways that require specific tools. There are regular bosses spread throughout each map, but the main combat loop comes from Mage hunts.

The hunts can be divided into 2 parts. First you accept a mission to hunt a specific Mage (there are a total of 21 different types) and proceed to chase it around the map. During this stage the Mage will rarely actively attack you and instead mainly spawn minions. Regular enemies, other Mages and their minions as well as hazeburned enemies (which only spawn while a hunt is active) will all be on the map alongside you and the Mage you're hunting. It's basically a free for all brawl. Once the Mage receives enough damage it will flee to another nearby spot on the map (always an open spot, they never spawn in tight hallways or something, the game has lots of big rooms) and if you repeat this enough times, you'll enter stage 2. The chase phase really isn't too troublesome, there is no time limit and you can even rest at a "bonfire" and restock your "Estus" in the middle of it without losing your progress. In the second stage you'll enter a proper boss fight with the Mage. Once you get close enough to it a health bar will pop up and walls will be erected around the area, so you can't run away or get punched off a platform. This is where the Mage will actively attack you with its full moveset and no longer spawn minions, although if minions or other enemies were present at the beginning of the fight they will stay there (and possibly fight the Mage alongside you). Mages as well as their minions drop materials specific to that kind of Mage, which you use to craft gear, same as in Monster Hunter.

The new DLC adds a 6th area to the game, but outside of a boss rush-style arena (which is an awesome addition) there are no Mages here, it's purely Souls-like in this case. You unlock it pretty early on, but it's kinda divided into 3 sub-areas with big difficulty differences, so I think you're meant to leave it after killing a sub-area boss and returning later once you're stronger. It's not a small DLC either, it has various hidden paths and a total of 4 bosses (one being a secret boss). The setting is probably my favourite in the entire game and it adds to the already very interesting lore. 

[h3]My Verdict[/h3]

Honestly, the only complaint I have about the game is the balancing in terms of raw numbers. At some point, roughly halfway through the game, everything seems to be turning into a glass cannon, both the player and the enemies. You start 1-3 hitting all the mobs and a lot of boss/Mage fights are over within less than a minute, whether you win or lose. Which is a shame, because some of them have cool movesets that would be rewarding to learn and master. But in practise you mostly end up killing them before they get use to all of it, or they kill you ofc. If everything did like half damage, the game would be more fun imo. Hopefully a future balancing patch will change this. Other than that, it's a nice game, with unique gameplay and a banger OST (the Mage theme goes hard). I have done 2 complete playthroughs so far and unlocked every achievement, all of which was a fun time. I definitely plan on making more characters in the future for various different builds as well.

I do still prefer Sanctuary and I do still hope that Ska's next game will be more like that again, but I don't think Sacrifice was a waste of a game. I would also like to see it getting a sequel at some point, ideally with better damage balancing and a bigger grind. Perhaps even higher difficulties/ranks, like in Monster Hunter, instead of NG+, since I found a bunch of fights, particularly the Mages, were too easy (but part of that was the damage you deal ofc).

Salt and Sacrifice is parts of Salt and Sanctuary, and Monster Hunter sandwiched together. With some interesting results.

+Art is unique and nice to look at. 
+Wide variety of bosses, both "Salt" bosses and "Mage" Bosses 
+Soundtrack is okay, minimalist if anything. Creature and sound effects are good.
+Hate it or love it. Salt has a way of ping ponging and throwing you off a cliff or send you flying 50 feet across the screen. Just laugh it off.
+Variety of craftable armour sets and weapons which are looted from the different mages across the game. Each with their own weights, stats, and art. Scythes, Daggers, Wands, Rods, Swords, Hammers 
+Hub world (I.E Dark Souls 3) , Shortcuts unlocked
+Quite a number of varied classes and an expansive talent tree.

Now for the negatives

-So, mage hunts are a mixed bag. When you start a mage hunt you follow a trail and start attacking the mage, he retreats and you follow along. Eventually you will box it in an arena and have to fight it. The game is usually generous with save points, with a small run back. Hate it or love it i guess is your call.
-Now even while you are on the hunt for a mage, there are other mages that can spawn on the map. This can be both frustrating and chaotic as you can either get dunked or the mages and their minions will attack each other.
-Biggest gripe I have with this system is, when you down a mage he kneels and you get a prompt to hit a button to "assassinate" it. Only issue is if there is an additional interactive element nearby you usually get drawn to that instead of killing the mage. This is a very case select scenario though. Just a small grievance I have.

Overall a different game than Salt and Sanctuary. Much less Dark Souls and Metroidvania, more Monster Hunter and semi linear gameplay route. Still has it's many moments of fun and two endings and new game +

Since the epic games launch this game has substantially improved in a lot but it still suffers from an identity crisis. 
It's a fusion of a Souls like and a Monster hunter like title ; in itself thats not the worst thing, but while trying to do both things equally it makes for an obtuse experience.

Mages attack patterns seem wildly inconsistent with very high damage and knockback attached where even the toughest armor still sees you losing half your health to something the mage easily filled the screen with and is still doing. Due to the gameplay loop being following mages across awkwardly small and perilous stages until you arrive at a small arena to be quickly filled with attacks , projectiles, roll catches-you name it. All mages feel about the same with little variety, which is a shame and a let down from both angles of souls like and MH-like.

Chase sequence take you through Souls-like maps which means filled with traps, lethal falls and high damage mobs; in later areas some mages will run away past a jump puzzle and once you reach it; it will run away back through it. When youre not dealing with hunting a mage, the maps themselves are decent the only issue with them is due to the comically strong knock back a lot of things have , youre often having to do that climb you just did all over again.

Oddly enough the most well designed fights that make for the better experience are not the mages, but the other non mage bosses such as the dragon. Having a dedicated stage thats spacious or having attack patterns that are telegraphed and have space between themselves to breath makes for a fun experience- who wouldve guessed.

Edit-The Final boss did play a different track this time? I had gone back to help a friend for the other ending and this time it wasnt the default mage theme. Probably a bug; leaving that bit in just incase someone else runs into it
Music is practically nonexistent ; Mages have 1 shared track which the final boss also uses.. Other bosses also have one shared track, most of the time you spend your time in awkward silence or very faint ambiance. Exceptions are the final map and a section of the new one they added in do have dedicated tracks but its strange seeing such a drastic drop in variety from the maker of Dishwasher series or Charlie murder. Its understandable wanting a more unsettling ambiance focused track for the tone and setting of the game- Monster Hunter World did not have constant tracks playing for every map for example; but that did have different battle themes for each map for small and big enemies alike- as well as a short intro theme for day and night- i had wished something similar was implemented as a break away from the unnerving silence.

I want to love this game, SKA and specifically James Silva has made my favorite games and favorite sound tracks , and maybe this didnt have to be Salt and Sanctuary 2 or even similar; its own thing as he clearly wanted it to be- but i do wish It leaned more in one direction or the other. Trying to do both as is just doesnt work for me; unfortunate as i love both genres but i cant recommend it. Do try SKAs other games though its predecessor Salt and Sanctuary i cannot recommend enough, and Dishwasher vampire smile has a killer sound track.

If you are looking to get this game because you liked Salt and Sanctuary, STOP AND READ first! This game is not like its predecessor. I'm currently 34 hours in and will only complete this game because I spent money on it. I don't find it fun or entertaining, just frustrating. So lets get into it.

Compared to Salt and Sanctuary:
It does not contain in interconnected world where you can move from on zone to another. It seems as though the developer abandoned their original ideas to follow those that are proven or expected to enhance game play. The sanctuary system is not in this game, preferring to use a dark souls 3 hub. It is the only place you can level, craft, enhance equipment, and move from one zone to another. There are NPC here, but outside of purchasing a few items they are almost entirely PVP based. Unlike the previous game NPC do not enhance, upgrade, or transmute gear. They literally included these NPC to talk to, dialog that is exhausted almost immediately. There are almost no eldritch horrors bosses to fight. The main point is to hunt down mages, that once defeated appear through out the zones mostly getting in your way. Which wouldn't be too bad if the only real way to gain experience wasn't gotten from them and them alone, mobs drop almost no EXP! Lastly, when compared, the interesting movement and and abilities from the previous game have been dropped in favor of those from other successful titles. Floating up wind currents, used in Ori, fail as they are never low enough and don't actually send you up is you have any horizontal momentum. Grappling hook, used in Halo, are basically uncontrollable and often send you flying where you are not trying to go. The grapple points are also often place horrifically. Magic is almost gone, this game uses weapon arts, like in Elden Ring.

As a stand alone game:
I think this game is just abysmal even when you don't compare it too Salt and Sanctuary. I would say that it can be described as either unrefined or rushed. Bosses are not really that unique, they all seem to fit in the exact same hit box with very generic differences, they all summon minions and their move sets are fairly easy to see. I think they were included in the world, outside boss arena, to solely pad out the game time. 34 hours is very long for a 2d game, especially when not fun, and I still haven't beat it. Control and movement wise, I use the same controller I use for every other game and this game just seems to have issues. It's not the very common occurrence of the game putting attacks exactly where you are dodging to, like input reading. Its not the dodge being too far to the point where it regularly puts you out of range to attack, especially when this game seem to seek to only ever give you enough time to strike once before you need to dodge again. The real issue is that it seems to loose button inputs. Need to grapple to avoid falling to you death, failed to accept button input. Trying to use a ranged weapon art requiring you to hold block then press Y, get interrupted and get locked into a stream of melee attacks that drain you stamina. Honestly, the thing that makes this game so frustrating to me is the damage sponge enemies, the utterly excessive traps with no visible triggers, and over reliance on enemies that that hid and pin you down to stab you to death.

The developers had such a good jump off point with Salt and Sanctuary, but for whatever reason abandoned it and ended with a game that was enticing but ultimately enjoyable.

Most negative reviews come from one of two places.
1. Close-minded people expecting this game to be exactly like the first. (Like how people hate Dark Souls 2 not because it was bad, but because it was different, and not what was expected)
2. People who got frustrated at one specific inconvenience/occurrence. (Like, say, getting mad that enemies were knocking them off ledges)

Salt and Sanctuary was 2D Dark Souls. Salt and Sacrifice is 2D Dark Souls AND 2D Monster Hunter. The game's formula follows Monster Hunter more closely than Dark Souls, but players of both games will feel right at home here.

I highly encourage you to look past the mixed reviews and try the game out for yourself. The game's mechanics are very streamlined and simple compared to most other games of its mixed genres. Even if you've never played Dark Souls, Monster Hunter, or any of Ska Studios' other titles, you can get into this game and have a good time. Because it's not a sequel and is barely related to Salt and Sactuary story wise as well, the game's story will not leave you with questions only answerable by another game.

All in all... I'm quite pleased by the gameplay, enemies, and all the hidden goodies I've been finding while exploring, I even fought some optional non-mage bosses last night that gave me a pretty good whipping before I beat them. 

 But yeah... I think alot of the hate may come from people who are upset that whatever cheesy tactic they used in the first game doesn't work in this one. That or they go in already poisoned by the toxic "this game sucks" sheeple mindset and only play the first area a bit--maybe a little bit of the second--see everything in a negative light, and proceed to parrot what they heard to everyone else. STOP DOING THAT!! 

 Another complaint I see is that the multiplayer community is too small or inactive... hilariously/sadly enough these complaints oftentime come from the same people who dump on the game and say how bad it is. No... If you want more players then stop telling everyone the game is bad. ESPECIALLY if you're still trying to play it. On that note, I would like to mention the pretty cool multiplayer options the game has, I've done a little bit of each so far and I can say I'm pleased with it.

> First off you have the classic Salt option: Co-op. Great experience! Vastly improved from the first game in that both players can get unique loot and progress their stories, special NPCs won't dismissively one-line you and not give you the pleasure of a full conversation. You can't invade or join other players' worlds in co-op mode, but you can still be invaded or joined yourself. Pretty cool.

> Next I'd like to mention the very souls-like "aid another player" and "ask for aid" options, they work great! You got candle things you can light when you are seeking to help somebody or looking for help and its pretty smooth sailing so long as whoever you connect to doesn't have a potato for a modem. There's also gestures available too! These are great, I always do a handshake and or a wave to somebody when I see them.

> Then you got the other souls-like "hunt a player" option. Same thing pretty much, you can light a candle saying you are seeking a challenger, or you can drop a cursed page to look for someone to invade. You meet, you fight, someone emerges victorious, bam.

> The last one is a very interesting one and probably my favorite: you have the runners and the sheriffs... Basically a game of cops and robbers; Runners invade other players' worlds to steal salt (exp) from the special enemies that spawn during mage hunts, in doing so they spawn dangerous enemies that attack the host player. Gather 9 times or kill the host and you win! If the mage hunt finishes before you do so however, you fail your mission. The sheriff players on the other hand are dedicated to entering worlds being pillaged by runners to hunt them down. Kill the runner to win!

> It is also worth noting that when you are in another player's world you have an indicator that regularly goes off that will show you the direction of your objective. If you're helping or trying to kill a player, the indicator shows you the direction of the host. If you're running, it will show you the direction of the sheriff, and the sheriff's indicator shows you the direction of the runner.

I definitely recommend this game to anybody who is a fan of the original and soulslike games in general. Its a shame the game released on Epic first... But now that its here on Steam it finally feels like its where it belongs. I look forward to an eventual third installment of the series if the hackjobs don't ruin its rep.
